Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## TRUTHFUL SELF-PLAY

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We present a general framework for evolutionary learning to emergent unbiased state representation without any supervision. Evolutionary frameworks such as self-play converge to bad local optima in case of multi-agent reinforcement learning in non-cooperative partially observable environments with communication due to information asymmetry. Our proposed framework is a simple modification of selfplay inspired by mechanism design, also known as _reverse game theory_ , to elicit truthful signals and make the agents cooperative. The key idea is to add imaginary rewards using the peer prediction method, i.e., a mechanism for evaluating the validity of information exchanged between agents in a decentralized environment. Numerical experiments with predator prey, traffic junction and StarCraft tasks demonstrate that the state-of-the-art performance of our framework.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Evolving culture prevents deep neural networks from falling into bad local optima (Bengio, 2012). For example, self-play (Samuel, 1967; Tesauro, 1995) has not only demonstrated the ability to abstract high-dimensional state spaces as typified by AlphaGo (Silver et al., 2017), but also improved exploration coverage in partially observable environments and communication (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2019) to exchange their internal representations, such as explored observation and hidden state in RNNs. Evolutionary learning is expected to be a general framework for creating superhuman AIs as such learning can generate a high-level abstract representation without any bias in supervision.

However, when applying evolutionary learning to a partially observable environment with noncooperative agents, improper bias is injected into the state representation. This bias originates from the environment. A partially observable environment with non-cooperative agents induces actions that disable an agent from honestly sharing the correct internal state resulting in the agent taking actions such as concealing information and deceiving other agents at equilibrium (Singh et al., 2019). The problem arises because the agent cannot fully observe the state of the environment, and thus, it does not have sufficient knowledge to verify the information provided by other agents. Furthermore, neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial examples (Szegedy et al., 2014) and are likely to induce erroneous behavior with small perturbations. Many discriminative models for information accuracy are available; these include GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Radford et al., 2016) and curriculum learning (Lowe et al., 2020). However, these models assume that accurate samples can be obtained by supervision. Because of this assumption, is it impossible to apply these models to a partially observable environment, where the distribution is not stable.

We generalize self-play to non-cooperative partially observable environments via mechanism design (Myerson, 1983; Miller et al., 2005), which is also known as _reverse game theory_ . The key idea is to add imaginary rewards by using the peer prediction method (Miller et al., 2005), that is, a mechanism for evaluating the validity of information exchanged between agents in a decentralized environment, which is calculated based on social influence on the signals. We formulate the non-cooperative partially observable environment as an extention of the _pertially observable stochastic games_ (POSG) (Hansen et al., 2004); introduce truthfulness (Vickrey, 1961), which is an indicator of the validity of state representation. We show that the imaginary reward enables us to reflect the bias of state representation on the gradient without oracles.

As the first contribution, we propose _truthful self-play_ (TSP) and analytically demonstrate convergence to the global optimum (Section 4). We propose the imaginary reward on the basis of the peer prediction

method (Miller et al., 2005) and apply it to self-play. The mechanism affects the gradient of the local optima, but not the global optima. The trick is to use _the actions taken by the agents_ as feedback to verify the received signal from the every other agent, instead of the true state, input, and intent, which the agents cannot fully observe. TSP only requires a modification of the baseline function for self-play; it drastically improves the convergence to the global optimum in Comm-POSG.

As the second contribution, based on the results of numerical experiments, we report that the TSP achieved state-of-the-art performance for various multi-agent tasks made of up to 20 agents (Section 5). Using predator prey (Barrett et al., 2011), traffic junction (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2019), and StarCraft Synnaeve et al. (2016) environments, which are typically used in Comm-POSG research, we compared the performances of TSP with the current neural nets, including the state-ofthe-art method, with LSTM, CommNet (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016), and IC3Net (Singh et al., 2019). We report that the model with IC3Net optimized by TSP has the best performance.

This work is the first attempt to apply mechanism design to evolutionary learning. TSP is a general optimization algorithm whose convergence is theoretically guaranteed for arbitrary policies and environments. Furthermore, since no supervision is required, TSP has a wide range of applications to not only game AIs (Silver et al., 2017), but also the robots (Jaderberg et al., 2018), chatbots (Gupta et al., 2019; Chevalier et al., 2019), and autonomous cars (Tang, 2019) that are employed in multiagent tasks.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Neural communication has gained attention in the field of _multiagent reinforcement learning_ (MARL) for both discrete (Foerster et al., 2016) and continuous (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2019) signals. Those networks are trained via self-play to exchange the internal state of the environment stored in the working memory of recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to learn the right policy in partially observable environments.

The term self-play was coined by the game AI community in the latter half of the century. Samuel (Samuel, 1967) introduced self-play as a framework for sharing a state-action value among two opposing agents to efficiently search the state space at Checkers. TD-Gammon (Tesauro, 1995) introduced self-play as a framework to learn TD( _λ_ ) (Sutton & Barto, 1998) and achieve professionalgrade levels in backgammon. AlphaGo (Silver et al., 2017) defeated the Go champion by combining supervised learning with professional game records and self-play. AlphaZero (Silver et al., 2018) successfully learnt beyond its own performance entirely based on self-play. All these studies explain that eliminating the bias of human knowledge in supervision is the advantage of self-play.

Self-play is also known as _evolutionary learning_ (Bengio, 2012) in the deep learning community mainly as an approach to emerging representations without supervision (Bansal et al., 2018; Balduzzi et al., 2019). Bansal et al. (2018) show that competitive environments contribute to emerging diversity and complexity. Rich generative models such as GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Radford et al., 2016) are frameworks for acquiring an environmental model by employing competitive settings. RNNs such as world models (Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Eslami et al., 2018) are capable of more comprehensive ranges of exploration in partially observable environments and generation of symbols and languages (Bengio, 2017; Gupta et al., 2019; Chevalier et al., 2019). The difference between evolutionary learning and supervised learning is the absence of human knowledge and oracles.

Several works have formalized those in which the agents exchange environmental information as a formal class of the games such as Dec-POMDP-Com (Goldman & Zilberstein, 2003) and COMMTDP (Pynadath & Tambe, 2002), and several frameworks are proposed to aim to solve the problems. However, the limitation of the frameworks is that they assume a common reward. As there are yet no formal definition of _non-cooperative_ communication game, we formalize such a game to

Comm-POSG as a superset of POSGs (Hansen et al., 2004), a more general class of multi-agent games including the cases of non-cooperativity (Hansen et al., 2004).

To the best of our knowledge, there are no studies that have introduced truthful mechanisms into the field of MARL, but it may be possible to introduce it by using agents that can learn flexibly, such as neural networks. A typical truthful mechanism is the VCG mechanism (Vickrey, 1961), which is a generalization of the pivot method used in auction theory, but whereas the subject of the report that must satisfy truthfulness must be a _valuation_ (or a value function if interpreted from a RL perspective). In this study, the scope of application is different because the belief states of the environment are subject to reporting. Therefore, we introduce instead a peer prediction method (Miller et al., 2005) that guarantees truthfulness with respect to reporting beliefs about arbitrary probability distributions using _proper scoring rules_ (Gneiting & Raftery, 2007).

## 3 PROBLEM DEFINITION

## 3.1 COMM-POSG

A _communicative partially-observable stochastic game_ (Comm-POSG) is a class of non-cooperative Bayesian games in which every agent does not fully observe the environment but interacts each other. We define Comm-POSG as an extension of POSG (Hansen et al., 2004) with a message protocol.
**Definition 3.1** (Hansen et al., 2004) POSG _⟨n, T, S, A, X , T , P, R⟩_ is a class for multi-agent decision making under uncertainty in which the state evolves over time 1 _≤ t ≤ T_ , where

- _n_ is the number of agents,

- _T_ is a horizon _i.e.,_ the episode length,

- _S_ is a set of discrete/continuous state _st ∈S_ with an initial probabilistic distribution _p_ ( _s_ 0),

- _A_ is a set of discrete/continuous action _ati ∈A_ ,

- _X_ is a set of discrete/continuous observation _xti ∈X_ ,

- _T ∈_ P ( _S × A × S_ ) is state transition probability,

- _P ∈_ P ( _S × X[n]_ ) is an observation probability, and

- _R_ : _S × A[n] →_ R _[n]_ is a reward function that outputs an _n_ -dimensional vector. □

In Comm-POSGs, every agent further follows a _message protocol Z[n][×][n]_ , where _Z_ is the discrete/continuous signal space. The complete information exchanged among the agent in time is **Z** _t_ , where **Z** _t_ := ( _ztij_ ) _i,j∈_ � _n_ � _∈Z[n][×][n]_ is a _signal matrix_ in which ( _i, j_ )-th entry _ztij_ represents a signal from Agent _i_ to Agent _j_ at _t_ . The _i_ -th diagonal entry of **Z** _t_ , _hti_ := _ztii_ represents the _pre-state_ , an internal state of _i_ -th agent before receiving the singals from the others. A game in Comm-POSG is denoted as _G_ := _⟨n, T, S, A, X , T , P, R, Z⟩_ .

The objective function of Comm-POSG is _social welfare_ (Arrow, 1963) defined by the following,

where _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] is discount rate, _rti_ is reward _πi_ is a stochastic policy, and _V[π][i]_ is the value function.

In extensive-form games including Comm-POSG, in addition to the information in the environment, the information of other agents cannot be observed. In the optimization problem under these assumptions, a policy converges to a solution called the Bayesian Nash equilibrium (BNE) (Fudenberg, 1993). We denote the social welfare at the BNE is _J[∗]_ , and the global maximum _J_[ˆ] . In general, _J[∗]_ = _J_[ˆ] holds, which is closely related to the information asymmetry.

Hence, the veracity of the signals **z** _ti_ is the point of contention.

## 3.3 TRUTHFULNESS

In mechanism design, a _truthful_ game (Vickrey, 1961) is a game in which all agents make an honest reporting in the Bayesian Nash equilibrium. In Comm-POSGs, the truthfulness of the game is achieved if all the sent signal equals the pre-state _ztij_ = _hti i.e.,_ all the agent share a complete information. In such case, all the agents can have the same information **ˆz** _ti_ = **h** _t_ := ( _ht_ 1 _, . . . , htn_ )[T] for all _i_ ’s. In such a case, all agents will have the same post-state model probability distribution and the mean of the cross entropy between the probability distributions of each model will be minimized.

The first term represents the entropy of knowledge each agent has about the environment, and the second term represents the information asymmetry between agents; _Dφ_ is a lower bound on the amount of true information the environment has, H [ _p_ ( _st_ )]; since achieving truthfulness is essentially the same problem as minimizing _Dφ_ , eventually it also maximizes _J[∗]_ will be maximized. **Proposition 3.1.** _(global optimally) For any games G in Comm-POSG, if Dφ_ ( **Z** _t_ ) = H [ _p_ ( _st_ )] _for_ 0 _≤ t ≤ T and Ri is symmetric for any permutation of i ∈_ � _n_ � _, J[∗]_ ( _G_ ) = _J_[ˆ] ( _G_ ) _holds_[1] _._

## 4 PROPOSED FRAMEWORK

An obvious way to achieve truthful learning is to add _Dφ_ as a penalty term of the objective, but there are two obstacles to this approach. One is that the new regularization term also adds a bias to the social welfare _J_ , and the other is that _Dφ_ contains the agent’s internal state, post-state _h_[ˆ] _ti_ , so the exact amount cannot be measured by the designer of the learner. If post-states are reported correctly, then pre-states should also be reported honestly, and thus truthfulness is achieved. Therefore, it must be assumed that the post-states cannot be observed during optimization.

Our framework, _truthful self-play_ (TSP), consists of two elements: one is the introduction of _imaginary rewards_ , a general framework for unbiased regularization in Comm-POSG, and the other is the introduction of peer prediction method (Miller et al., 2005), a truthful mechanism to encourage honest reporting based solely on observable variables. In the following, each of them is described separately and we clarify that the proposed framework converges to the global optimum in Comm-POSG. We show the whole procedure in Algorithm 1.

## 4.2 PEER PREDICTION MECHANISM

The _peer prediction mechanism_ (Miller et al., 2005) is derived from a mechanism design using _proper scoring rules_ (Gneiting & Raftery, 2007), which aims to encourage verifiers to honestly report their beliefs by assigning a reward measure score to their responses when predicting probabilistic events. These mechanisms assume at least two agents, a reporter and a verifier.

The general scoring rule can be expressed as _F_ ( _ps∥s_ ) where _ps_ is the probability of occurrence reported by the verifier for the event _s_ , and _F_ ( _ps∥s_ ) is the score to be obtained if the reported event _s_ actually occurred. The scoring rule is _proper_ if an honest declaration consistent with the beliefs of the verifier and the reported _ps_ maximizes the expected value of the earned score, and it is _strictly proper_ if it is the only option for maximizing the expected value. A representative example that is strictly proper is the logarithmic scoring rule _F_ ( _ps∥s_ ) = log _ps_ , where the expected value for a 1-bit signal is the cross-entropy _p[∗] s_[log] _[ p][s]_[+ (1] _[ −][p][∗] s_[) log(1] _[ −][p][s]_[)][ for belief] _[ p][∗] s_[. One can find that] _[ p][s]_[=] _[ p][∗] s_[is the] only report that maximizes the score.

Since the proper scoring rule assumes that events _s_ are observable, it is not applicable to problems such as partial observation environments where the true value is hidden. Miller et al. (2005), who first presented a peer prediction mechanism, posed scoring to a posterior of the verifiers that are updated by the signal, rather than the event. This concept is formulated by a model that assumes that an event _s_ emits a signal _z_ stochastically and infers the type of _s_ by the signal of the reporters who receive it. The peer prediction mechanism (Miller et al., 2005) is denoted as _F_ ( _p_ ( _s|z_ ) _∥s_ ) under the assumption that ( **1** ) the type of event _s_ and the signal _z_ emitted by each type follow a given prior, ( **2** ) the priors are shared knowledge among verifiers, and ( **3** ) the posterior is updated according to the reports.

We apply the mechanism to RL, _i.e.,_ the problem of predicting the agent’s optimal behavior _ati ∼ πθ|st_ for the true state _st ∈S_ . In self-play, the conditions of **1** and **2** can be satisfied because the prior _πθ_ is shared among the agents, and furthermore, the post-state in Comm-POSG corresponds to **3** , so that the peer prediction mechanism can be applied to the problem of predicting agent behavior. To summarize the above discussion, we can see that we can allocate a _score matrix Lt_ as follows,

## 4.3 THE TRUTHFUL SELF-PLAY

In our framework, a truthful mechanism is constructed by introducing the proper prediction mechanism into imaginary rewards. Since the score matrix _Lt_ does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtracting the mean of the scores from each element of the matrix, thereby making the sum to zero. ## 5 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENT

In this section, we establish the convergence of TSP through the results of numerical experiments with deep neural nets. We consider three environments for our analysis and experiments ( _→_ Fig. 1). ( **a** ) a predator prey environment (PP) in which predators with limited vision look for a prey on a square grid. ( **b** ) a traffic junction environment (TJ) similar to Sukhbaatar et al. (2016) in which agents with limited vision learn to signal in order to avoid collisions. ( **c** ) StarCraft: Brood Wars (SC) explore and combat tasks which test control on multiple agents in various scenarios where agent needs to understand and decouple observations for multiple opposing units.

We compare the performances of TSP with self-play (SP) and SP with curiosity (Houthooft et al., 2016) using three tasks belonging to Comm-POSG, comprising up to 20 agents. The hyperparameters are listed in the appendix. With regard CRAs, three models namely LSTM, CommNet (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016), and IC3Net (Singh et al., 2019), were compared. The empirical mean of the social welfare function _J_ was used as a measure for the comparison. IC3Net is an improvement over CommNet, which is a continuous communication method based on LSTM. Actor-critic and value functions were added to the baselines in all the frameworks. We performed 2,000 epochs of experiment with 500 steps, each using 120 CPUs; the experiment was conducted over a period of three days.

**PP and TJ:** Table 1 lists the experimental results for each task. We can see that IC3Net with TSP outperforms the one with SP for all tasks. Fig. 2 (a) shows that TSP elicits truthful information, and (b) confirms that the social welfare of TSP exceeds that of the SPs. (c) confirms that the average of the imaginary part is zero. From these experimental results, we conclude that the TSP successfully realized truthful learning and state-of-the-art in tasks comprising 3 to 20 agents.

## 6 CONCLUDING REMARK

Our objective was to construct a general framework for emergent unbiased state representation without any supervision. Firstly, we proposed the TSP and theoretically clarified its convergence to the global optimum in the general case. Secondly, we performed experiments involving up to 20 agents and achieved the state-of-the-art performance for all the tasks. Herein, we summarize the advantages of our framework.
1. **Strong convergence** : TSP guarantees convergence to the global optimum theoretically and experimentally; self-play cannot provide such a guarantee. Besides, the imaginary reward _**i**_ ∆ _L_

2. **Simple solution** : The only modification required for TSP is that _**i**_ ∆ _L_ should be added to the baseline in order to easily implement it for deep learning software libraries such as TensorFlow and PyTorch.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an interesting application of mechanism design (peer prediction) to multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to enforce truthfulness in communication. While the empirical results across several environments are promising and the conceptual framework is novel for the ICLR audience, there are significant technical ambiguities and potential mathematical inconsistencies in how the 'imaginary rewards' are integrated into the gradients. Specifically, the definition of the imaginary unit $i$ within complex-valued rewards and its subsequent transformation into real-valued weight updates via a 'mass parameter' $ eta$ is underspecified, making the core methodology difficult to replicate or theoretically verify.

comments.title: Ambiguity in complex-valued reward gradient
comments.quote: \Delta \mathbf{w} = \alpha \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \sum_{t=1}^T (\text{Re } \mathbf{R}_t^+ + \beta \text{Im } \mathbf{R}_t^+)
comments.explanation: The authors introduce imaginary rewards using the imaginary unit $i$. In standard complex analysis and optimization, one usually optimizes a real-valued objective. Here, the authors use a 'mass parameter' $ eta$ to collapse the complex reward into a real gradient. However, the update rule in step 4 of Algorithm 1 suggests $(\text{Re } \mathbf{R}_t^+ + \beta \text{Im } \mathbf{R}_t^+)$ is treated as a scalar reward for a policy gradient. Since $\mathbf{R}_t^+$ is defined as a matrix (Eq 8: $\mathbf{R} + i\Delta L$), it is unclear how the gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}$ is applied to a matrix sum without a trace or a per-agent summation. Usually, REINFORCE is applied to the scalar return of an agent. If the imaginary rewards are meant to be 'zero-sum' across agents (matching the Hermitian claim), the sum over agents $i$ of the imaginary parts should be zero, potentially rendering $\beta \text{Im } \mathbf{R}_t^+$ zero in the aggregate gradient if they share weights (as stated in Section 3.2). This makes the mechanism's effect on the gradient non-trivial and underspecified.

comments.title: Conflict between matrix definition and scalar reward usage
comments.quote: Combine the real and imaginary rewards, $\mathbf{R}_t^+ = \mathbf{R}_t + i \Delta L_t$
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1, $\mathbf{R}$ is defined as a diagonal matrix with reward $r_i$ as the $(i,i)$-th entry. $\Delta L_t$ is a Laplacian-filtered score matrix. However, standard policy gradient (Step 4 in Algorithm 1) requires a scalar reward $R(\tau)$ for each agent or a joint reward. If $\mathbf{R}_t^+$ is a matrix, the notation $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \sum (\text{Re } \mathbf{R}_t^+ ...)$ is mathematically inconsistent with standard RL notation where rewards are scalars. The text does not explicitly state which component of the matrix $\mathbf{R}_t^+$ is used to update which agent's policy, or if it's the trace of the matrix. While I can infer they might use the $i$-th diagonal element for the $i$-th agent, the formal notation remains ambiguous.

comments.title: Questionable claim of global optimality
comments.quote: TSP converges to the global optimum $\hat{J}$ if the following convergence condition are met, $\text{Im } \Delta L \to D_\phi$
comments.explanation: The authors claim Theorem 4.1 proves convergence to the global optimum. In MARL, 'global optimum' usually refers to the social welfare maximum. While the peer prediction mechanism can theoretically incentivize truthfulness (making the game 'truthful'), a truthful game does not automatically guarantee that agents will reach the globally optimal policy (the best possible coordinated behavior), only that they will report their internal beliefs honestly. The proof sketch relies on the game becoming truthful, but truthfulness is a necessary, not sufficient, condition for solving the coordination problem to reach $\hat{J}$, especially in complex environments like StarCraft. The claim of reaching the global optimum for 'arbitrary policies and environments' is likely an overstatement.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency with imaginary unit
comments.quote: $\_\textbf{i}\_$ is the imaginary unit. ... Vectors are columns. ... $\_\textbf{i}\_$ is a ( $n - 1$)-tuple obtained by removing the $i$-th entry
comments.explanation: In the 'Notation' section, the authors define a bold-italic **i** as the imaginary unit, but immediately after define $\mathbf{a}_{-i}$ (using a standard subscript $i$) and then use $i$ as a general index elsewhere. In Section 4.1, they acknowledge the potential confusion: 'Note the use of $i$ for imaginary units and $i$ for indices'. However, in the formula for $L_t$ (Eq 6) and the gradient update (Algorithm 1), the visual distinction between the index $i$ and the imaginary unit $i$ is almost non-existent in the provided text, which could easily mislead a reader or lead to implementation errors.

comments.title: Unclear definition of the scoring rule $F$
comments.quote: $\ell ( a_{ti} | z_{tji} ) := F ( \pi_\theta ( a_{ti} | z_{tji} ) \parallel a_{ti} ) = \log \pi_\theta ( a_{ti} | z_{tji} )$
comments.explanation: The score matrix $L_t$ uses the signal $z_{tji}$ (signal from $j$ to $i$) to evaluate the action $a_{ti}$. The authors state this is a peer prediction method. However, in the standard peer prediction (Miller et al., 2005), the reward is usually based on the correlation between two agents' reports. Here, the reward is calculated using the policy $\pi_\theta$. If the policy is still learning, $\pi_\theta$ is a moving target. The paper does not sufficiently justify why using the current (potentially incorrect) policy as the 'proper scoring rule' baseline leads to a stable equilibrium, rather than a degenerate one where the policy and signals collapse to a simple, uninformative agreement.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents an ambitious and conceptually novel framework—Truthful Self-Play (TSP)—that combines mechanism design with multi-agent reinforcement learning to promote truthful communication in partially observable environments. The integration of peer prediction and imaginary rewards is creative and well-motivated. The theoretical framework is largely sound, and the experiments demonstrate strong empirical performance. However, several technical and logical issues in the derivation and presentation undermine the rigor of the claims, particularly around the use of complex rewards, convergence conditions, and the formulation of the peer prediction mechanism. While the core idea has merit, the current presentation contains ambiguities and unjustified steps that must be clarified to support the strong convergence claims.

comments.title: Misuse of imaginary unit as reward basis
comments.quote: Imaginary rewards are virtual rewards passed from an agent and have a different basis _**i**_ than rewards passed from the environment, with the characteristic that they sum to zero.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the use of the imaginary unit _**i**_ as a basis for virtual rewards seems like a notational trick to separate real and imaginary components. The authors intend to use complex-valued rewards where the real part is environmental and the imaginary part is peer-predicted, with the constraint that imaginary rewards sum to zero across agents. However, treating rewards as complex numbers introduces substantial mathematical issues. In standard RL, rewards are real-valued, and value functions are derived from expected sums of real scalars. There is no standard interpretation of a complex reward, nor is it clear how policy gradients would be computed on a complex domain without additional structure (e.g., Wirtinger calculus). Moreover, the paper does not clarify how the "real structure" of the network is maintained (p. 5), nor does it justify why complex arithmetic doesn't distort the learning dynamics. While the Hermitian condition ensures the imaginary part of total reward is zero, this doesn't resolve the fundamental issue that gradients of log-probabilities with respect to complex rewards lack a standard interpretation. The idea of offsetting internal rewards is sound (e.g., as in difference rewards), but the complex-number formulation appears to be a misleading formalism that could mislead readers into thinking a well-established mathematical framework is being used, when in fact it is ad hoc and underspecified.

comments.title: Incorrect application of peer prediction to policy evaluation
comments.quote: _Lt_ := ( _ℓ_ ( _ati|ztji_ )) _i,j∈_  _n_ ; _ℓ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) := _F_ ( _πθ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) _∥ati_ ) = log _πθ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) _,_ (6)
comments.explanation: The peer prediction mechanism is intended to reward agents for truthful reporting of beliefs. However, in Equation (6), the score is defined as the log-likelihood of agent _i_'s action _ati_ under agent _j_'s policy conditioned on _ztji_, which is agent _j_'s received message. This conflates two different concepts: (1) reporting a belief over environment states or signals, and (2) observing an action and evaluating its likelihood under a policy. In true peer prediction (Miller et al., 2005), agents report their posterior beliefs about a hidden event, and are scored based on the coherence between reports. Here, agents are not reporting beliefs, but the score is computed based on actions taken and received messages. There is no evidence that _πθ(ati|ztji)_ represents agent _j_'s belief about the true state or signal—rather, it is a policy conditioned on its own post-state. Furthermore, _ztji_ is not a belief but a message (possibly a raw hidden state). Without a mapping from messages to beliefs or a mechanism for eliciting belief reports, the application of proper scoring rules is invalid. The method effectively scores agent _i_ based on how predictable its action was given agent _j_'s internal state, which does not incentivize truthful communication of internal states as claimed. This misapplication undermines a core mechanism of TSP.

comments.title: Ambiguity in truthfulness condition and Proposition 3.1
comments.quote: In Comm-POSGs, the truthfulness of the game is achieved if all the sent signal equals the pre-state _ztij_ = _hti i.e.,_ all the agent share a complete information.
comments.explanation: The definition of truthfulness is ambiguous. It states that _ztij = hti_ for all _i,j_, meaning every agent sends its pre-state to all others. But this is only 'truthful' if the sender intends to reveal its pre-state—there's no mechanism yet in place to enforce or define incentives for this behavior at this point in the paper (that comes later). More importantly, Proposition 3.1 claims that if _Dφ_(**Z**_t_) = H[p(st)] and rewards are symmetric, then _J∗ = Ĵ_. But _Dφ_ is described as a lower bound on true information, and its equality to H[p(st)] implies perfect knowledge of the environment state, which is generally impossible in partially observable settings. The proposition conflates truthful communication with full observability. Even if agents truthfully share all internal states, they may still be collectively uncertain about the true environment state due to partial observability. Therefore, minimizing information asymmetry between agents does not imply convergence to the global optimum unless full observability is recovered, which is not guaranteed. The proposition overreaches by suggesting that truth in communication alone leads to global optimality, without accounting for the fundamental limitations of partial observability.

comments.title: Unjustified zero-averaging via graph Laplacian
comments.quote: we perform zero-averaging by subtracting the mean of the scores from each element of the matrix, thereby making the sum to zero. This can be expressed as follows using the graph Laplacian ∆:= _E −_ **11**[T] _/n_ . \ _**i**_ **Y** ← _**i**_ ∆ _L_
comments.explanation: The paper claims that applying the graph Laplacian ∆ to the score matrix _L_ ensures that the total imaginary reward sums to zero. However, ∆ is defined as _E - 11ᵀ/n_, which is an _n×n_ matrix, while _L_ is also _n×n_. The operation ∆L is a matrix product, not a mean-subtraction operation applied elementwise or row/column-wise. The correct way to zero-center a matrix would be to subtract the overall mean or apply centering to rows/columns (e.g., using _H = I - 11ᵀ/n_). The Laplacian ∆ is typically used in graph theory to encode connectivity and is not standard for matrix centering. The expression _**i** Y ← **i** ∆ L_ results in a matrix where each row is adjusted by the row average (if ∆ were applied incorrectly), but this does not ensure that the sum of all entries of _Y_ is zero unless _L_ has symmetric or zero-sum properties. This derivation lacks justification, and the misuse of the graph Laplacian as a centering operator suggests a misunderstanding of its purpose. A correct zero-sum constraint would require _∑ᵢⱼ yᵢⱼ = 0_, but the current formulation does not guarantee this.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for pre-state and post-state
comments.quote: - _fφ_ ( _h_[ˆ] _t−_ 1 _,i, xti_ ) _→Z_ is a deep RNN ... - _hti_ := _fφ_ ( _h_[ˆ] _t−_ 1 _,i, xti_ ),
comments.explanation: The paper uses both _h_[ˆ]_t-1,i and _hti_ to denote states, with the hat suggesting an estimate. However, in the definition of the communication protocol, _hti_ is described as the 'pre-state', which is the output of _fφ_ before receiving messages. Then, _qφ_ updates the 'post-state' given received messages. But in the algorithm (line 5), it says 'Update pre-state _hti ← fφ_ ( _h_[ˆ] _t−_ 1 _,i, xti_ )', while previously _fφ_ outputs to _Z_, the signal space. This suggests a conflation between the hidden state of the RNN and the communication signal. Furthermore, the notation switches between _h_ and _ĥ_ without a consistent pattern. For example, the post-state is denoted _ĥ_ti in one place but later updated as _hti ← ..._ in the algorithm. This inconsistency risks confusion about what is being communicated vs. what is internal, and whether the pre-state _hti_ is equal to the communication signal _z_ti or a separate entity. While OCR artifacts may contribute (_[ˆ]_ likely being a corrupted hat), the repeated use across sections suggests a notation problem.

comments.title: Underspecified convergence condition in Theorem 4.1
comments.quote: if the following convergence condition are met, \ _‖θ − θ∗‖ < β_
comments.explanation: The convergence condition in Theorem 4.1 is given as _‖θ − θ∗‖ < β_, but _θ∗_ is never defined in the paper. It is unclear whether _θ∗_ denotes an optimal parameter, a fixed point, or a truthful equilibrium. The mass parameter _β_ is described as _β ≥ 0_, but for the inequality to be meaningful, _β_ must be sufficiently large. However, the paper states _β < ∞_ as if that were a sufficient condition. Moreover, the condition implies convergence only when the current parameters are already close to _θ∗_, which suggests local convergence rather than global. This contradicts the claim of global optimality. The proof summary refers to Proposition A.3, which is not included in the provided text, making it impossible to verify. The convergence condition is therefore underspecified and circular: it assumes proximity to a solution to prove convergence to that solution. A proper convergence analysis would need to establish conditions under which the dynamics lead to truthfulness from arbitrary initialization.

comments.title: Misleading claim about state-of-the-art performance
comments.quote: Numerical experiments with predator prey, traffic junction and StarCraft tasks demonstrate that the state-of-the-art performance of our framework.
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically incorrect (likely 'demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance'), but more importantly, the claim is overstated. The experiments compare only LSTM, CommNet, and IC3Net with and without TSP, and show that IC3Net with TSP outperforms the others on the tested tasks. However, there is no comparison to other state-of-the-art MARL methods such as QMIX, COMA, or recent attention-based communication models. The baselines are relatively old (2016–2019), and no ablation is provided to isolate the effect of TSP from the choice of IC3Net. While the results are promising, claiming 'state-of-the-art' without broader comparison overreaches. The conclusion is more accurately that TSP improves performance over standard self-play in the tested models and domains, not that it sets a new SOTA across MARL.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an innovative framework combining mechanism design with multi-agent reinforcement learning to address information asymmetry in non-cooperative environments. The core idea of using peer prediction mechanisms to encourage truthful communication is interesting and potentially valuable. However, the paper suffers from several technical issues that undermine its claims, including overoptimistic theoretical guarantees, unclear mathematical constructions, and contradictions between claimed and reported results. The experimental evaluation shows mixed results, with TSP performing worse than baselines in some StarCraft tasks despite claims of state-of-the-art performance. The mathematical foundations around imaginary rewards and convergence conditions need clarification and correction.

comments.title: Overoptimistic claim in Proposition 3.1
comments.quote: Proposition 3.1. (global optimally) For any games G in Comm-POSG, if Dφ(Z_t) = H[p(s_t)] for 0 ≤ t ≤ T and Ri is symmetric for any permutation of i ∈ ⟦n⟧, J[∗](G) = J[ˆ](G) holds
comments.explanation: The authors claim that with minimized information asymmetry and symmetric rewards, the Bayesian Nash equilibrium equals the global optimum. This is not generally true in non-cooperative games even with perfect information - coordination problems can prevent global optimality. The proof is referenced to Section A but not shown in context. Without additional assumptions (e.g., specific reward structures or coordination mechanisms), this appears to be an overstatement.

comments.title: Unclear Hermitian property enforcement
comments.quote: The whole imaginary reward is denoted[2] as **i**Y = (**i** yij) i,j∈⟦n⟧ where **i** yij is the imaginary reward passed from Agent i to Agent j
comments.explanation: For Proposition 4.1 to hold, **R**[+] must be Hermitian, requiring yij = -yji for all i,j. However, the construction via peer prediction and Laplacian in Algorithm 1 doesn't explicitly ensure this antisymmetric property. The connection between ∆L operation and antisymmetry needs clarification - it's not clear why applying the graph Laplacian to the score matrix yields an antisymmetric matrix.

comments.title: Problematic convergence condition
comments.quote: Theorem 4.1. (global optimally) For any games in Comm-POSG, TSP converges to the global optimum J[ˆ] if the following convergence condition are met, lim_{T→∞} β/T = 0
comments.explanation: The condition lim_{T→∞} β/T = 0 is either trivial (if β is constant) or underspecified (if β varies with T). As T (episode length) goes to infinity, β/T naturally approaches 0 for any fixed β, making the condition meaningless. If β is meant to be a function of T, this should be explicitly stated. The condition appears either always satisfied or ill-defined.

comments.title: Score matrix construction issue
comments.quote: Lt := (ℓ(ati|ztji)) i,j∈⟦n⟧; ℓ(ati|ztji) := F(πθ(ati|ztji) ∥ati) = log πθ(ati|ztji)
comments.explanation: The score matrix entry uses agent i's action (ati) to evaluate the signal from j to i (ztji). This seems misaligned with peer prediction - if evaluating j's signal to i, we should use j's policy or signal properties, not i's action. The construction doesn't clearly implement the goal of evaluating signal veracity between agents.

comments.title: Contradiction in StarCraft results
comments.quote: Table 2: StarCraft Results: TSP beats the baselines... Explore-10Medic 50×50: CommNet 1.592±0.169, IC3Net 2.276±1.427, IC3Net w/ TSP 1.364±0.107
comments.explanation: The text claims TSP beats baselines, but the numbers show IC3Net w/ TSP performs worse (1.364) than both CommNet (1.592) and IC3Net (2.276). Similar pattern appears in Combat task. This contradicts the claim of state-of-the-art performance and raises questions about the experimental evaluation.

comments.title: Missing baseline in policy gradient
comments.quote: Update the weights by policy gradient (Williams, 1992), ∇θ V[π]i ← ∇θ (rti + **i** yti)
comments.explanation: The policy gradient update shown is missing the baseline term typically used in REINFORCE to reduce variance. While imaginary rewards may act as a baseline, the gradient expression should explicitly include it (e.g., value function) for completeness. Without it, the gradient estimator would have high variance.

comments.title: Underspecified mass parameter β
comments.quote: The real and imaginary parts of the complex rewards are ordered by the mass parameter β during training
comments.explanation: The role of β is not clearly explained - how it balances real vs imaginary rewards, its effect on dynamics, or guidance for setting its value. It appears in the convergence condition but its practical impact is not discussed, making the method harder to reproduce.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents an interesting framework, 'Truthful Self-Play' (TSP), which adapts the peer prediction mechanism from game theory to address information asymmetry in multi-agent reinforcement learning. The core intuition—using imaginary rewards to incentivize honest communication—is conceptually elegant. However, the paper suffers from significant issues in technical reporting, specifically regarding the derivation and definition of the imaginary reward mechanism and the mathematical consistency of the proposed Laplacian-based scoring rule. Several key steps in the methodology are underspecified or mathematically opaque, particularly the transition from the abstract definition of 'imaginary reward' to the specific implementation of 'zero-averaging' using the Laplacian, which complicates the reproducibility of the method.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Zero-Averaging Definition
comments.quote: This can be expressed as follows using the graph Laplacian \Delta:= E − \textbf{11}[T] /n. \begin{equation} \textbf{i} \textbf{Y} = \textbf{i} \Delta L_t \end{equation} to get \begin{equation} \textbf{Y} = (E - \frac{\textbf{11}^T}{n})L_t \end{equation}
comments.explanation: There is a formal ambiguity here. The text defines the Laplacian $\Delta$ as $E - \frac{\textbf{11}^T}{n}$. If $L_t$ is an $n \times n$ matrix, then $\Delta L_t$ represents the zero-centering of the rows of $L_t$ (i.e., subtracting the row mean). However, the definition of an imaginary reward matrix to maintain zero-sum property typically requires the matrix to be skew-symmetric rather than just zero-row-summing. If $\textbf{Y} = \Delta L_t$, the column sums are not necessarily zero, nor is $\textbf{Y}$ guaranteed to be Hermitian (or skew-Hermitian) such that the system-wide social welfare is invariant. The paper claims this construction ensures the social welfare is unaffected, but the step from row-centering to an overall zero-sum property is not justified.

comments.title: Underspecified Implementation of Imaginary Rewards
comments.quote: The real and imaginary parts of the complex rewards are ordered by the mass parameter \beta during training... 3. Combine the real and imaginary rewards, \begin{equation} \textbf{R}_{combined} = \textbf{R} + \beta \textbf{Y}_{imaginary} \end{equation}
comments.explanation: The text refers to a 'mass parameter \beta'. While Algorithm 1 mentions initialization of $\beta \ge 0$, the paper does not explain how the gradients are effectively backpropagated through a 'complex' reward space. Since standard RL infrastructure (e.g., PyTorch/TensorFlow) operates on real-valued tensors, it is unclear if the agent is being trained on the real part of the gradient, the norm, or some concatenation. The phrase 'ordered by the mass parameter' is imprecise and does not detail the mechanics of how the imaginary component influences the policy optimization loop.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Peer Prediction Condition
comments.quote: The peer prediction mechanism (Miller et al., 2005) is denoted as F(p(s|z) \parallel s) under the assumption that ( 1 ) the type of event s and the signal z emitted by each type follow a given prior, ( 2 ) the priors are shared knowledge among verifiers, and ( 3 ) the posterior is updated according to the reports.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that (2) is satisfied because the policy $\pi_\theta$ is shared. However, in non-cooperative games (as defined in the abstract), agents may have different hidden states or beliefs. If agents are non-cooperative, assuming that the 'prior' (the policy) is enough to satisfy the requirements of the peer prediction mechanism—which typically requires strict alignment of belief updates to ensure truthfulness—is a major logical leap that is not sufficiently addressed.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel framework, Truthful Self-Play (TSP), that integrates peer prediction from mechanism design into self-play to encourage truthful communication among agents in partially observable environments. The core idea of using imaginary rewards to align individual incentives with global objectives is interesting. However, the manuscript contains several critical issues: missing mathematical formulations due to OCR/formatting errors that hinder reproducibility, a fundamental conceptual tension between modeling the environment as 'non-cooperative' while optimizing a 'social welfare' objective, and non-standard notation for the imaginary unit that complicates theoretical claims. Additionally, the claim that the method merely modifies the baseline function misrepresents the algorithmic changes. Addressing these logical inconsistencies, clarifying the mathematical derivations, and providing the missing equations would significantly strengthen the paper's rigor and clarity.

comments.title: Conceptual tension: Non-cooperative setting vs. Social Welfare maximization
comments.quote:
A _communicative partially-observable stochastic game_ (Comm-POSG) is a class of non-cooperative Bayesian games in which every agent does not fully observe the environment but interacts each other.

The objective function of Comm-POSG is _social welfare_ (Arrow, 1963) defined by the following,
comments.explanation: The authors define Comm-POSG as a 'non-cooperative' game, yet the stated objective is to maximize 'social welfare' $J$ and converge to its global optimum $\hat{J}$. In standard multi-agent reinforcement learning and game theory, non-cooperative agents optimize individual rewards, which often conflict with the social welfare maximum. The paper claims that truthful signaling guarantees $J^* = \hat{J}$ (Proposition 3.1), but this assumes that aligning individual information sharing automatically aligns individual incentives with the global social welfare function in a non-cooperative equilibrium. Without a rigorous proof showing how the mechanism design overcomes the fundamental misalignment of incentives in non-cooperative settings, this claim is theoretically unjustified and contradicts standard MARL literature where social welfare is typically an analysis metric, not an agent objective.

comments.title: Missing objective function and algorithmic equations
comments.quote:
The objective function of Comm-POSG is _social welfare_ (Arrow, 1963) defined by the following, 

 where _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] is discount rate, _rti_ is reward

2. Compute a score matrix _Lt_ with the peer prediction mechanism,

3. Combine the real and imaginary rewards,

4. Update the weights by policy gradient (Williams, 1992),
comments.explanation: Several key equations are missing from the text, likely due to PDF extraction errors. The explicit formula for the social welfare objective function $J$ is absent, making it impossible to verify the normalization or discounting factors used. Furthermore, Algorithm 1 (Steps 2, 3, and 4) lacks the precise mathematical definitions for computing the score matrix, combining rewards, and the exact policy gradient update rule including the imaginary reward term. Without these equations, the proposed method cannot be reproduced or technically verified. The convergence condition for Theorem 4.1 is also missing. These omissions severely impact the paper's technical completeness.

comments.title: Non-standard and confusing notation for the imaginary unit
comments.quote:
_**i**_ is the imaginary unit. Re _u_ and Im _u_ are a real and an imaginary part of complex number _u_ , respectively.

The whole imaginary reward is denoted[2] as _**i**_ **Y** = ( _**i**_ _yij_ ) _i,j∈_  _n_
comments.explanation: The notation section explicitly defines boldface **i** as the imaginary unit. In mathematical literature, the imaginary unit is standardly denoted as $i$ or $\mathrm{i}$, never in boldface, as bold usually denotes vectors or matrices. The footnote in Section 4.1 attempts to clarify the distinction between bold **i** (imaginary) and regular $i$ (index), but using bold for a scalar imaginary unit is highly non-standard and creates immediate confusion with vector notation (e.g., **i**$Y$ could be misread as a vector-matrix product). Additionally, Proposition 4.1 claims the complex reward matrix $\mathbf{R}^+$ is Hermitian. For $\mathbf{R}^+ = \mathbf{R} + i\mathbf{Y}$ to be Hermitian, the diagonal entries $i y_{ii}$ must be real, implying $y_{ii}$ are purely imaginary, which contradicts the typical interpretation of rewards as real-valued quantities. This notation choice obscures the mathematical claims.

comments.title: Misleading claim about baseline modification
comments.quote: TSP only requires a modification of the baseline function for self-play; it drastically improves the convergence to the global optimum in Comm-POSG.
comments.explanation: The introduction states that TSP 'only requires a modification of the baseline function'. In policy gradient methods (e.g., REINFORCE), a baseline is used solely for variance reduction and does not change the expected gradient or the target policy. However, TSP introduces a completely new reward component (the imaginary reward derived from peer prediction) and adds it to the environment reward. Adding a reward term fundamentally changes the objective function being optimized, altering the target stationary distribution, rather than merely acting as a variance-reducing baseline. This phrasing understates the algorithmic contribution and could mislead readers about the nature of the method.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing of the peer prediction score matrix
comments.quote:
we can allocate a _score matrix Lt_ as follows, 
_Lt_ := ( _ℓ_ ( _ati|ztji_ )) _i,j∈_  _n_ ; _ℓ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) := _F_ ( _πθ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) _∥ati_ ) = log _πθ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) _,_ (6) which is an _n_ -order square matrix representing the score from Agent _i_ to Agent _j_ .
comments.explanation: The authors describe $L_t$ as 'the score from Agent $i$ to Agent $j$', but the entry $\ell(a_{ti} | z_{t_{ji}})$ explicitly depends on $z_{t_{ji}}$ (the signal sent by agent $j$ to agent $i$) and $a_{ti}$ (the action of agent $i$). This represents the score *for* agent $j$ *regarding* agent $i$'s action, or a prediction score where rows index predicted actions and columns index predicting agents. The phrasing 'score from $i$ to $j$' reverses the natural direction of the signal and reward flow, creating ambiguity about how the matrix is actually used in the reward combination step (which is also missing). Clarifying the row/column semantics and the direction of the reward signal is necessary for reproducibility.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes Truthful Self-Play (TSP), an interesting attempt to integrate mechanism design with multi-agent reinforcement learning to encourage truthful communication. However, the theoretical core contains a critical mathematical error regarding the Hermitian property of the constructed reward matrix, which undermines the claimed convergence guarantee. Furthermore, the experimental results contain a conspicuous sign inconsistency (tables show positive numbers while text states scores are negative). The application of the peer prediction mechanism to reinforcement learning is not sufficiently justified for the given setting, and the proof of Theorem 4.1 is too sketchy to be convincing even with the appendix. These issues significantly reduce the paper's reliability.

comments.title: Erroneous claim that R + iΔL is Hermitian
comments.quote: R + iΔL is Hermitian; and if Eq (9) holds, then G[iΔL] is truthful from Proposition A.3.
comments.explanation: The paper defines Δ = I - (1/n)11^T (a centering matrix) and sets the imaginary reward matrix to iΔL. For R + iΔL to be Hermitian, ΔL must be skew-symmetric. However, for a general L, ΔL is not skew-symmetric (e.g., in the 2x2 case it is skew-symmetric only if special conditions hold). The Hermitian condition is essential for Proposition 4.1, which requires the complex reward matrix to be Hermitian so that the imaginary part of total social welfare vanishes. Since this condition is not generally satisfied, the theoretical guarantee of convergence to the global optimum is unsupported.

comments.title: Inconsistent signs in experimental results
comments.quote: All the scores are negatives. The experiment was repeated thrice. The average and standard deviation are listed. **Bold** is the highest score.
comments.explanation: Both Table 1 and Table 2 display positive numbers (e.g., 1.92±0.35, 1.592±0.169), but the text explicitly states that all scores are negative. The environment rewards (e.g., -0.05 per step) also imply cumulative totals should be negative. This suggests minus signs are missing from the table entries, which would mislead readers about the relative performance and the direction of improvement. This is a clear inconsistency between the stated sign and the displayed values.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for peer prediction in RL setting
comments.quote: ℓ(a_ti|z_tji) := F(π_θ(a_ti|z_tji)∥a_ti) = log π_θ(a_ti|z_tji)
comments.explanation: The peer prediction mechanism requires that the verifier's (agent i) action be a stochastic function of only the reporter's signal for the scoring rule to be proper. In a Comm-POSG, agent i's action also depends on its own observation and other signals, not just on the signal from agent j. The paper assumes shared priors, but does not account for these additional dependencies that break the conditional independence assumption needed for truthfulness. The deferred proof (Appendix A) would need to address this, but the main text provides no argument that the mechanism is indeed truthful under the given game structure.

comments.title: Ambiguous convergence condition and sketchy proof of Theorem 4.1
comments.quote: Theorem 4.1. (global optimally) For any games in Comm-POSG, TSP converges to the global optimum J^ if the following convergence condition are met, ... Proof (in summary.)
comments.explanation: The convergence condition (Eq 9) is not clearly stated in the excerpt, and the proof is dismissed in a single sentence with references to the appendix. Given the strong claim of convergence to the global optimum, the main text should at least outline the condition and the key steps. The current presentation makes it impossible for a reader to assess the validity without the appendix, and even with it, the brevity raises concerns about rigor.

comments.title: Misnamed 'graph Laplacian' for centering matrix
comments.quote: using the graph Laplacian Δ:= E − 11^T /n
comments.explanation: The matrix I - (1/n)11^T is typically called the centering matrix. While it is proportional to the Laplacian of a complete graph (nI - 11^T) scaled by 1/n, calling it a 'graph Laplacian' without qualification is unconventional and could confuse readers.

comments.title: Imprecise statement about policy gradient baseline
comments.quote: The baseline function in policy gradient (Williams, 1992) is an example of a function that does not affect the objective function when the mean gets zero.
comments.explanation: A baseline in policy gradient does not bias the gradient estimate regardless of its mean, as long as it does not depend on the action (Williams, 1992). The condition 'when the mean gets zero' is not necessary and is misleading; any constant baseline works, but the mean property is related to variance reduction, not unbiasedness.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper introduces an interesting theoretical intersection between mechanism design (specifically peer prediction) and multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to address information asymmetry and convergence issues in non-cooperative settings. The core idea of using 'imaginary rewards' to enforce truthful signaling is novel and theoretically grounded. However, the execution of this idea reveals significant deficiencies. The notation is fundamentally ambiguous due to a conflict between the imaginary unit and agent set notation. More critically, the mathematical derivation for ensuring the 'imaginary reward' matrix is Hermitian (a necessary condition for the convergence proof) relies on a 'zero-averaging' step that does not actually enforce the required skew-symmetry. Furthermore, the algorithm description is underspecified regarding how the score matrix is converted into a scalar reward vector for each agent, making the proposed method difficult to implement. These issues constitute a major gap between the theoretical ambition and the reproducibility of the framework.
